questions.question: In Paxos, what does an Acceptor promise during Phase 1 (Prepare)?
options: To accept any future proposal regardless of number
Not to accept any proposal numbered less than the received proposal number
To broadcast the proposal to all other acceptors
To commit the proposed value to persistent storage immediately
questions.explanation: When an acceptor responds to a Prepare(n), it promises not to accept any proposal numbered less than n. This guarantee is what connects different rounds — any later proposer that reaches a quorum will discover any previously accepted value because at least one acceptor in the overlapping quorum will report it.

questions.question: Why must any two quorums overlap by at least one acceptor?
options: To reduce the number of messages needed for consensus
To ensure that at least one acceptor knows which value was chosen or seen most recently, preventing conflicting decisions
To guarantee that network latency is bounded
So that the proposer can detect when a majority is unreachable
questions.explanation: Quorum overlap is the foundation of Paxos's safety guarantee. If quorum Q1 chose value V, and a new proposer later reaches quorum Q2, at least one acceptor in Q2 was also in Q1. That acceptor will report V during Phase 1, forcing the new proposer to use V — never a different value.

questions.question: A proposer completes Phase 1 and receives promises from a quorum. One acceptor reports it previously accepted (n=5, value='X'). No other acceptor reports any previously accepted value. What value must the proposer propose in Phase 2?
options: The proposer's own originally intended value
The value with the highest proposal number from Phase 1 responses, which is 'X'
The value proposed by the acceptor with the lowest node ID
A new value that is a merge of 'X' and the proposer's value
questions.explanation: Paxos requires that the proposer use the value from the highest-numbered previously accepted proposal reported during Phase 1. Since 'X' was accepted at n=5 and no other accepted value was reported, the proposer must propose 'X' — not its own value. This is the mechanism that guarantees a chosen value can never be changed.

questions.question: What is the 'dueling proposers' problem in Paxos?
options: Two proposers propose different values and both get chosen, violating safety
Two proposers keep incrementing proposal numbers and preempting each other, preventing either from completing Phase 2
Two acceptors refuse all proposals, making progress impossible
Two learners receive different values and cannot reconcile them
questions.explanation: Dueling proposers is a liveness problem, not a safety problem. When two proposers alternate (P1 prepares with n=1, P2 prepares with n=2, P1's accept is rejected, P1 prepares with n=3, P2's accept is rejected, ...), neither can complete Phase 2 because the other keeps invalidating promises. Safety is never violated — no wrong value is chosen — but no value is chosen at all.

questions.question: How does designating a 'distinguished proposer' (leader) solve the dueling proposers problem?
options: The leader can accept proposals without waiting for a quorum
The leader skips Phase 1 entirely, reducing message count
Only one proposer runs at a time, so there is no competition to preempt each other and Phase 2 completes
The leader has a special veto power over other proposers' proposal numbers
questions.explanation: A distinguished proposer is the only node that proposes values. Without a competing proposer to issue higher-numbered prepare requests, the leader's Phase 1 promises are never invalidated, so Phase 2 completes. The leader doesn't skip any phases or bypass quorum requirements — it just eliminates the competition that caused the livelock.

questions.question: In a 5-node Paxos cluster, nodes A, B, and C (a quorum) have accepted value V at proposal number 5. A new proposer runs Phase 1 with proposal number 10. What happens?
options: The new proposer can choose any value it wants because its proposal number is higher
At least one of A, B, or C will be in the new quorum and will report V in its Phase 1 response, forcing the proposer to use V
The acceptors reject the new proposal because V is already chosen
The new proposer must wait for all 5 acceptors to respond before proceeding
questions.explanation: Since any two quorums overlap by at least one acceptor, at least one of {A, B, C} will respond to the new proposer's Phase 1. That acceptor will report that it accepted V at proposal number 5. The proposer must use V — the highest-numbered previously accepted value from Phase 1 responses — not its own proposed value. Higher proposal numbers give priority in the protocol, not the freedom to change a chosen value.
